FAQ - Buying Real Estate in Saudi Arabia

Frequently asked questions about real estate property for sale and foreign investment in Saudi Arabia

Can foreigners buy real estate in Saudi Arabia?

Yes! Saudi Arabia now allows foreign investors and residents to buy property. The updated foreign real estate ownership system permits non-Saudis to own properties in designated geographic zones across Riyadh, Jeddah, Madinah, and Makkah.

Who is eligible to buy real estate in Saudi Arabia?

Four main categories can benefit: (1) Legal residents with valid residence permits for personal use housing, (2) Investors purchasing property worth SAR 4M+ for premium residency, (3) Foreign companies needing properties for operations, and (4) Investors seeking commercial and industrial real estate.

Where can foreigners buy property in Saudi Arabia?

Designated zones in Riyadh include: Al-Qadia, New Square, KAFD, King Salman Airport. Jeddah includes 55 development zones. Madinah zones: Downtown Madinah, Knowledge City. Makkah zones: Abraj Makkah, Jabal Umar, Makkah 1&2. Premium zones in holy cities available for qualified investors.

What are the fees for foreign property ownership?

The system imposes transaction fees not exceeding 5% of the property value. Penalties for violations can reach 5% of the transaction value, or up to SAR 10 million for providing false information to obtain ownership rights.

How do I apply for foreign property ownership?

All procedures are available electronically through the "Saudi Real Estate" portal, Absher platform, and the Real Estate Authority. Submit required documents including property details, ownership deed, and financial eligibility proof. Applications are reviewed and processed within a short timeframe.

What are the investment benefits for foreign buyers?

The system aims to attract international developers and investors, boost residential, commercial, industrial, and tourism sectors, create employment opportunities, and enhance property project quality. Premium residency investors get permanent residence, free movement rights, and up to 99-year usufruct in holy cities.
